coredata.dc:title: Room temperature phosphorescent materials based on rosin acid with time-dependent afterglow and visible light excitation

coredata.dc:description: Room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials based on carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention due to their potential applications in anti-counterfeiting in recent years. However, it remains a considerable challenge to prepare CDs-based RTP materials with sunlight excitation and excellent stability. This work provides a straightforward method for one-step calcination of rosin-based carbon quantum dots (R-CDs) with aluminum hydroxide (Al(OH)3) to create ecologically sustainable room-temperature phosphorescent materials (CDs@Al2O3). CDs@Al2O3 exhibit excitation dependence, emitting green phosphorescence under 365 nm excitation, yellow phosphorescence under visible light excitation, and blue-green phosphorescence under sunlight excitation. Surprisingly, CDs@Al2O3 display time-dependent afterglow, switching from blue to green phosphorescence when excited at 285 nm. Furthermore, after being submerged in polar and acid-base solutions for 21 days, the phosphorescence of CDs@Al2O3 is still visible to the unaided eye. At last, CDs@Al2O3 composites are utilized in applications including smart flashing traffic signals, multi-level information, and anti-counterfeiting. This work expands creative application opportunities by offering a straightforward and adaptable approach to generating ecologically friendly RTP materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Sunlight excitation
Visible light excitation
Afterglow time-dependence
Strong stability
Multifunctional applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs and CDs@Al2O3 composites
Conditions and time of solar excitation testing
Morphology and chemical composition of CDs@Al2O3
Application of CDs and CDs @ Al2O3 composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM picture of R-CDS (the lattice of R-CDS is shown inset). (b) magnified image a). (c) FT-IR spectra and (d) Raman spectrogram of R-CDS. (e) XPS measurements of R-CDS, high-resolution (f) c 1s, (g) N 1s, (h) O 1s, and (i) S 2p. (j) UV absorption, excitation, and emission spectra and k) Fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs. (l) R-CDs emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
TEM image of (a) 500-CDs@Al2O3, (b) 600-CDs@ Al2O3, (c) 700-CDs@ Al2O3. (d) Corresponding Al, O, C, N, and S in 500-CDs@Al2O3 elemental mapping images.

(a, b) XRD of calcined R-CDs@Al(OH)3 at various temperatures (100–700 °C). (c) FT-IR spectral profiles and (d) XPS survey spectrums of CDs@Al2O3 composites. (e–i) high-resolution (e) C 1s, (f) O 1s, (g) N 1s, (h) S 2p and (i) Al 2p XPS spectrum of 500-CD@Al2O3. (j) DSC traces, (k) TG spectra, (l) Schematic representation of the proposed structure of 500-CD@Al2O3.

Photographs of CDs@Al2O3 powders after (a) solar excitation, (b) UV excitation, visible excitation, and shutdown. Phosphorescence emission spectra excited at different excitation wavelengths of (c) 500-CDs@Al2O3, (e) 600-CDs@Al2O3 and (g) 700-CDs@Al2O3. Phosphorescence lifetime plots of (d) 500-CDs@Al2O3, (f) 600-CDs@Al2O3 and (j) 700-CDs@Al2O3. (h) UV–Vis absorption and phosphorescence excitation spectra and i) phosphorescence lifetime diagrams at different delay times of 700-CDs@Al2O3.

(a–c) delayed emission spectra of cds@al2O3 at different temperatures. Afterglow decay curves of (d) 500-CDs@Al2O3 (collected at 450 emission), (e) 600-CDs@Al2O3 (collected at 500 nm emission) and (f) 700-CDs@Al2O3 (collected at 425 nm emission). The upper right inset showed the trend of the lifetime as a function of temperature. (g) Photographs of 500-CDs@Al2O3 dispersed in different solvents. Phosphorescence spectra of 500-CDs@Al2O3 dispersed in different (h) pH solutions and (i) different solutions.

Spectrograms of (a) 500-CDs@Al2O3, (b) 600-CDs@Al2O3 and (c) 700-CDs@Al2O3 at 77 k were determined. (d) EPR spectra of CDs@Al2O3. (e) The HOMO and LUMO energy levels were calculated for CDs and CDs@Al2O3. (f) Schematic of the energy level structure of the CDs@Al2O3 material.

Application of CDs and CDs@Al2O3 composites. Anti-counterfeiting of alphabetic graphics of (a) 500-CDs@Al2O3, (b) CDs and 600-CDs@Al2O3, (c) 700-CDs@Al2O3 graphics. (d) CDs/Rh6G@Al2O3 and (e) 600-CDs@Al2O3 in fingerprinting. (f) QR code was scanned to obtain encrypted information. (g) Light-activated RTP ‘bowtie’. (h) Application scenario of prefabricated flashing traffic signs.
Schematic representation of the fabrication process of R-CDs and R-CDs@Al2O3 composites.

head.ce:title: Room temperature phosphorescent materials based on rosin acid with time-dependent afterglow and visible light excitation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry & Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Central South University of Forestry & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry & Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials based on carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention due to their potential applications in anti-counterfeiting in recent years. However, it remains a considerable challenge to prepare CDs-based RTP materials with sunlight excitation and excellent stability. This work provides a straightforward method for one-step calcination of rosin-based carbon quantum dots (R-CDs) with aluminum hydroxide (Al(OH)3) to create ecologically sustainable room-temperature phosphorescent materials (CDs@Al2O3). CDs@Al2O3 exhibit excitation dependence, emitting green phosphorescence under 365 nm excitation, yellow phosphorescence under visible light excitation, and blue-green phosphorescence under sunlight excitation. Surprisingly, CDs@Al2O3 display time-dependent afterglow, switching from blue to green phosphorescence when excited at 285 nm. Furthermore, after being submerged in polar and acid-base solutions for 21 days, the phosphorescence of CDs@Al2O3 is still visible to the unaided eye. At last, CDs@Al2O3 composites are utilized in applications including smart flashing traffic signals, multi-level information, and anti-counterfeiting. This work expands creative application opportunities by offering a straightforward and adaptable approach to generating ecologically friendly RTP materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials were able to continue to emit light for a period of time after the excitation light source was switched off, with lifetimes on the order of milliseconds visible to the naked eye. RTP materials were used in bio-imaging [1–3], encryption [4,5], anti-counterfeiting [6–8], and optoelectronic devices [9–11] because of their long lifespan, large Stokes shift [12–14], and decreased interference from background fluorescence. Early research of RTP materials focused on complexes related to inorganic luminescent materials, but inorganic luminescent materials suffered from the problems of toxicity of heavy metals, limited supply from the earth, high prices and complicated preparation processes [15]. Therefore, it was crucial to design an RTP material that was simple to synthesize, environmentally friendly, and inexpensive.

Biomass carbon quantum dots (Bio-CDs) were one of the main approaches to addressing the issues of toxicity, high cost, and environmental unfriendliness of RTP materials. Bio-CDs were defined as small carbon nanoparticles with a size of approximately 10 nm or less [16–18]. Various biomasses have been used as carbon sources for the preparation of Bio-CDs, such as rice husks [19], ginkgo leaf [20], grasses [21], and so on. Rosin acid, as one of the biomasses, was a tricyclic diterpenoid compound.It had a bicyclic structure, which could inhibit the motion of excited-state molecules, increase aggregation-induced emission (AIE), and promote non-radiative decay [22]. Maleic rosin was a derivative of rosin acid, which could provide more C=O groups. And C=O groups were the basis for phosphorescence generation, so maleic rosin had good potential as a carbon source for the preparation of CDs [23].

Recently, Bio-CDs in embedded matrix or other matrices enable room temperature phosphorescence [24–26]. However, due to triple-state exciton inactivation caused by oxygen and water molecules, the majority of RTP CDs in aqueous solution caused phosphorescence quenching and could not tolerate the extreme conditions [27]. Li et al. obtained CD/BA by a one-step thermal decomposition of a simple mixture of amino acids and boric acid, which can be characterized by a color-tunable RTA behavior under visible light excitation. However, CD/BA cannot withstand extreme conditions [28]. Secondly, the excitation sources for RTP CDs materials were ultraviolet light (UV), visible light and sunlight. Visible light (380–780 nm) and sunlight were more advantageous than ultraviolet light in practical applications because they were easier to obtain, had greater penetration power, and had lower phototoxicity. Zhang et al. achieved yellow phosphorescence excited in the visible by doping g-CDs prepared from 2.4-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid into an alumina matrix, yet were limited to excitation sources in the UV and visible regions [29]. Solar-excited room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials were mainly focused on metal-doped long afterglow materials, such as Y3Ga5O12:Bi3+, SrAl2O4:Eu2+, Dy3+, Sr3MgSi2O8:Eu2+, Dy3+, and so on. However, the high cost of precursors of these materials and the high toxicity of some of them have largely limited their practical applications [30–32]. Moreover, the performance of the described RTP CDs materials in advanced information encryption was limited because RTP CDs typically produced static phosphorescent color outputs. Therefore, it was necessary to prepare RTP CDs with non-toxic, strong stability, visible light excitation, sunlight excitation and afterglow time dependence in a simple manner.

In this work, maleic rosin and thiourea were used to make CDs. Thiourea contained N and S atoms. N, S atoms co-doping could tune the energy band structure of CDs, decrease the band gap, and increase the visible absorption [33]. An alumina matrix was used to give a stiff structure, and a one-step calcination process was successfully used to create CD@Al2O3 composites with strong stability, afterglow time-dependent and visible excitation. The alumina matrix could adsorb R-CDs through spatial confinement effects and inter- or intramolecular interactions to adsorb R-CDs, thereby reducing vibration and rotation and stabilizing their triplet excitation to produce long afterglows. CDs@Al2O3 emitted green phosphorescence when activated at 365 nm, bright yellow phosphorescence when activated by visible light, and blue-green phosphorescence when excited by sunlight. CDs@Al2O3 exhibited an afterglow time dependence after the removal of 285 nm UV light, and its phosphorescence color changed from blue to green. In addition, CDs@Al2O3 showed superior stability in acid-base solutions and polar solutions. This work expanded the area of creative applications by offering a straightforward and adaptable method for creating novel RTP materials.

All the chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and were used directly upon receipt without further purification. Maleic rosin was supplied by Hunan Songlin Technology Co. (Hunan, China). Thiourea, aluminum hydroxide, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, dimethyl sulfoxide, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), hydrogen peroxide, trichloromethane, acetone, and rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China), and ethanol (99.5 %) was purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co. Deionized water was used in all experiments, and the deionized water was purified by the Water Purifier Nano water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs and CDs@Al2O3 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 described the synthetic procedure of R-CDs and CDs-Al2O3. To put it briefly, ethanol solution (20 mL) was mixed with maleic rosin (0.50 g) and thiourea (0.28 g), which were then ultrasonically dissolved in the solution for 15 min [7]. The resulting ethanol solution was then heated in an autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h, purified, and freeze-dried to create the desired R-CDs, which were then stored at 4 °C. Second, Al(OH)3 (2.0 g) was added to R-CDs (0.1 g) and deionized water (5 mL) to create a mixture, which was then sonicated for 15 min to dry it [29]. It was then placed in a muffle furnace with a temperature increase of 5 °C/min to 500 °C, 600 °C and 700 °C, and it was calcined for two hours to produce CDs@Al2O3 composites. Names for the acquired CDs@Al2O3 were 500-CDs@Al2O3, 600-CDs@Al2O3, and 700-CDs@Al2O3, depending on the calcination temperature.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained on a JEOL-2100 microscope. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrometer. Raman spectra were obtained on a Lab Ram HR Evolution. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. UV absorption and transmission spectra were measured using a Hitachi UV-3600 spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured using a Hitachi FL-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence decay curves, afterglow emission spectra, and afterglow decay curves were recorded using a FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was acquired on a Rigaku Smart Lab SE in Japan. Differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) spectra were obtained on a TA DSC25 differential scanning calorimeter, USA. Thermogravimetric (TG) was obtained on a Rigaku Thermogravimetric Analysis, Japan. Temperature-dependent PL emission spectra were measured using a FLS-980. EPR spectra were recorded using an EPR spectrometer (Lauda Scientific LSA100, Germany). All optical images and videos were recorded on a Xiaomi 14 smartphone from China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditions and time of solar excitation testing
ce:section.ce:para: The detection time was set at noon on a sunny day in early March. The testing site was located at the Central South University of Forestry Science and Technology (Changsha, China, 112°59′E, 28°11′N). The samples were placed in a darkroom setup under sunny weather conditions and fully exposed to sunlight. After 3 min of exposure, a video was taken using a smartphone to record the duration of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) produced by sunlight excitation that was observable to the naked eye.

R-CDs were systematically characterized through various tests. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to examine the R-CDs' size and lattice structure. Their lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, matching the (100) crystallographic plane of graphite (Fig. 1
a,b). [34] The average particle size of the R-CDs was 3.48 nm (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of R-CDs showed broad vibrational bands in the range of 3600–3100 cm−1 that could be attributed to N–H or O–H stretching vibrations. Stretching of C–H, C=O, C=S, and C–N can be linked to the characteristic strong peaks at 2918, 1697, 1480, and 1385 cm−1 (Fig. 1c) [35]. C=O bonds promoted ISC and SOC development in R-CDs, which in turn promoted afterglow emission. The Raman spectrum of R-CDs had two peaks at 1371 and 1587 cm−1, representing graphite D and the crystalline G band associated with sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of R-CDs was 0.86, indicating the presence of a disordered graphitic structure in R-CDs (Fig. 1d) [36]. The interpretation of the FT-IR spectra was supported by the results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which indicated that the R-CDs were mainly composed of the elements C, N, O, and S (Fig. 1e). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum exhibited five characteristic peaks at binding energies of 284.8 eV (sp2-hybridized carbon, C=C bonds), 285.2 eV (sp3-hybridized carbon, C–C bonds), 285.8 eV (C-N bonds), 286.8 eV (C=S bonds), and 289.1 eV (C=O bonds) (Fig. 1f). According to the XPS peak fitting results, the peak area ratio of sp2-hybridized carbon (284.8 eV) was significantly higher than that of sp3-hybridized carbon (285.2 eV), indicating that sp2-hybridized carbon was the predominant structural component in the material [37]. It was further demonstrated that the material has a graphitized ordered structure with a small number of defects or disordered regions. The O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 1g showed that the O atoms were mainly present in the form of two peaks at 532.1 (O=C–H) and 533.1 eV (C=O). The high-resolution spectrum of the N 1 s band showed that there were two peaks at 400.7 and 402.6 eV, which were mainly in the form of pyrrolic N and N-O (Fig. 1h) [38]. C-S-C (S 2p3/2), C-S-C (S 2p1/2), and C=S bonds corresponded to 163.9, 165.1, and 169.0 eV in the high-resolution S 2p spectra in Fig. 1I [39]. The results of thermogravimetric analysis showed that the R-CDs had excellent thermal stability (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The optical characteristics of R-CDs were examined following chemical and structural characterization. Fig. 1j displayed the UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs, in the 200–300 and 300–400 nm bands, which correspond to the C=C π-π* and C=O n-π* leaps [40]. The R-CDs were excited at the optimal excitation wavelength of 425 with an emission wavelength of 517 nm and emitted green fluorescence at an excitation of 365 nm. According to the following equation: [41]
(1)
τ
ave
= ∑
a i
τ
i
2
/ ∑
a i
τ i

When excited at 425 nm, the optimal excitation wavelength, the R-CDs produced green fluorescence at a wavelength of 518 nm with a fluorescence lifetime of 5.48 ns (Fig. 1k and Table S1, Supporting Information) [42]. As shown in Fig. 1l, when R-CDs were excited at 280 nm, they released blue fluorescence at 422 nm. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity showed a gradual decreasing trend. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was 6.23 % when excited at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical composition of CDs@Al2O3
ce:section.ce:para: A thorough structural characterization of the three composites was done in order to illustrate the special afterglow characteristics of the 500-CDs@Al2O3,600-CDs@Al2O3 and 700-CDs@Al2O3 composites. The generated R-CDs were embedded in the Al2O3 matrix, as shown by TEM images. The elements C, N, O, S, and Al were present in samples 500/600/700-CDs@Al2O3, according to the electron microscope mapping (Fig. 2 ). The carbon quantum dots were the source of elements C, N, and S, while Al(OH)3 was the source of most elements Al and O. The elements S and N lost some of their contents due to the volatiles that were released during the calcination process.

To better understand the cross-linking mode of CDs with Al2O3 matrix, the XRD of R-CDs@Al(OH)3 composites was examined at various temperatures (Fig. 3
a,b). R-CDs@Al(OH)3 preserved its original chemical structure when operating at a temperature below 200 °C; at a temperature above 200°C, it started to lose its H2O molecules and changed from a hydroxide to an AlOOH intermediate with a bilayer structure. The appearance of Al2O3 diffraction peaks at temperatures above 500 °C suggested that AlOOH was gradually changing into a dense Al2O3 lattice structure. The R-CDs were fully embedded and securely fixed in the small Al2O3 space, which was in agreement with the TEM results.

The chemical structures of the composites were characterized using FT-IR and XPS. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra showed that CDs@Al2O3, in addition to containing abundant functional groups of CDs, exhibited significant absorption peaks in the range of 803 cm−1–591 cm−1 (Fig. 3c), which could be attributed to the vibrational modes of the Al-O bond. This finding suggested a strong interaction between the Al2O3 matrix and the CDs [43]. The XPS spectra in Fig. 3d–i showed that all three composites contained the same five elements: C, O, N, S, and Al. At 284.8 eV, 532.8 eV, 400.4 eV, 152.9 eV, and 119.0, 74.4 eV, these elements were placed [44]. It was determined that the two peaks in the high-resolution Al 2p, located at 75.6 eV and 74.8 eV, were caused by Al-O-C and Al-O/Al=O vibrations. The results demonstrated the formation of covalent bonds between R-CDs and the Al2O3 matrix.

Differential scanning calorimetric analysis was performed to better understand the structure of the composites (Fig. 3j). The results demonstrated that during the heating phase, the composites of 500-CDs@Al2O3, 600-CDs@Al2O3, and 700-CDs@Al2O3 exhibited a notable heat absorption transition at 62.3 °C, 53.6 °C and 61.5 °C. This change, which might have been caused by the hydrogen bonds between the dots breaking. After calcination at 700 °C, some of the hydrogen bonds in the compounds formed by CDs and aluminum hydroxide might be broken due to the increase in temperature. In order to verify the existence of hydrogen bonds in 700-CDs@Al2O3, it was analyzed by variable temperature infrared spectroscopy under a vacuum environment. The results showed that the characteristic absorption peak of the hydroxyl group (–OH) was red-shifted with the increase of temperature, which suggested the existence of hydrogen bonding in 700-CDs@Al2O3 (Fig. S3, Supporting Information) [45]. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was used to evaluate the thermal stability of the three composites. Thermogravimetric analysis showed that the three composites still exhibited good thermal stability at high temperatures above 700 °C, and their mass residuals were maintained at more than 93 %, with the mass loss of CDs@Al2O3 being caused by the loss of water molecules (Fig. 3k) [46]. The reason for these differences may be that the water molecules in the Al2O3 matrix were decreasing as the calcination temperature increases, resulting in less heat absorption and more stability at higher temperatures. Under the same preparation conditions (0.1 g CDs, calcination temperature 500 °C, 600 °C, and 700 °C for 2 h), the residual rate of CDs was significantly reduced, consistent with the results of their thermogravimetric analyses (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). This phenomenon suggested that when Al(OH)3 was used as the substrate, the in situ generated Al2O3 during the calcination process formed a physical encapsulation effect on the carbon dots, which effectively inhibited the pyrolytic loss of R-CDs as the luminescent centers [47]. According to the previously described structural analysis, functional groups such as –COOH, –NH, and –OH were widely distributed on the surface of the R-CDs. The R-CDs were connected to Al2O3 through C-O-Al bonds, and a hydrogen bonding structure was formed between the functional groups on the surface of the R-CDs and the small amount of –OH groups present in Al2O3. As a result, the Al2O3 matrix effectively immobilized the R-CDs through spatial confinement effects and inter- or intramolecular reactions (e.g., C-O-Al covalent bonding and hydrogen bonding.) This immobilization not only significantly confined the triplet excited states of the R-CDs, but also effectively suppressed their intramolecular vibrational and rotational degrees of freedom, which resulted in a significant enhancement of the photophysical stability of the R-CDs in the composites. Fig. 3l showed the possible chemical binding and structural interaction between the R-CDs and the Al2O3 matrix.

CDs@Al2O3 composite powders exhibited a bright yellow phosphorescence when stimulated under visible light and a brilliant green phosphorescence when activated at UV 365 nm. Notably, CDs@Al2O3 composite powders exhibited distinct green phosphorescence when exposed to sunlight excitation. This finding not only expanded the range of excitation light sources for CDs@Al2O3, but also provided new experimental evidence for its potential in outdoor photoluminescence applications. 500-CDs@Al2O3 and 600-CDs@Al2O3 emitted blue phosphorescence upon activation at 285 nm. Unexpectedly, under excitation at a wavelength of 285 nm, 700-CDs@Al2O3 exhibited a persistent luminescence time, transitioning from blue phosphorescence to green phosphorescence within 3 s, making it easier to observe with the naked eye (Fig. 4
a). Furthermore, 500-CDs@Al2O3 observed RTP under the same conditions of excitation, whereas the calcined pure 500-Al2O3 matrix showed almost no afterglow emission. This indicated that the phosphorescence did not come from the Al2O3 (Fig. S5, Supporting Information). As the excitation wavelength was changed from 254 nm to 365 nm, the phosphorescence emission strength of 500-CDs@Al2O3 and 600-CDs@Al2O3 gradually reduced, with a noticeable red shift of the characteristic emission peaks (Fig. 4b, d). The phosphorescence quantum yield of 500-CDs@Al2O3 was 10.26 %, and the phosphorescence quantum yield of 600-CDs@Al2O3 was 12.58 %. Lifetimes of 500-CDs@Al2O3 at 360 nm excitation and 435 nm and 479 nm monitoring were 101.1 ms and 221.1 ms (Fig. 4c and Table S2, Supporting Information). At an excitation wavelength of 254 nm, the lifetime of 600-CDs@Al2O3 was 289 ms (Fig. 4e and Table S3, Supporting Information). Under 254 nm excitation, the afterglow lifetimes of 500-CDs@Al2O3 and 600-CDs@Al2O3 were 1.517 and 1.520 s (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). The phosphorescence spectra showed that at different excitation wavelengths, 700-CDs@Al2O3 had two emission bands located in the blue region centered at 420 nm and in the green region centered at 500 nm (Fig. 4f and Fig. S7, Supporting Information). When the phosphorescence emission wavelength was set at 425 nm, the structure showed that the excitation peak at 267 nm and the absorption band at 269 nm significantly overlapped. This suggested that the blue phosphorescence originated from the n-π* jump in the carbon nucleus of the conjugated sp2 cluster. When the wavelength of phosphorescence emission was set at 500 nm, the excitation peak at 315 nm was enhanced and matched the absorption peak at 317 nm. This implied that the green phosphorescence and the surface state of the carbon dots were closely related (Fig. 4g). The phosphorescence quantum yield of 700-CDs@Al2O3 was 8.09 % when excited at 365 nm. The time-resolved phosphorescence spectra of 700-CDs@Al2O3 after excitation at 285 nm demonstrated that the intensity of the blue phosphorescence diminished faster than that of the green phosphorescence with time (Fig. 4h). 700-CDs@Al2O3 had lifetimes of 10.59 ms and 48.48 ms at wavelengths of 420 nm and 500 nm (Fig. 4i and Table S4, Supporting Information). The results demonstrated that there was a dynamic transition from blue to green phosphorescence, with the intensity of the blue phosphorescence decaying more quickly and the green phosphorescence gradually gaining the upper hand over time. Furthermore, 400-CDs@Al2O3 and 800-CDs@Al2O3, which exhibited nearly negligible phosphorescence under 365 nm excitation, were synthesized in order to further ascertain the ideal calcination temperature (Fig. S8 and S9, Supporting Information). After calcination at 400 °C for 2 h, the CDs@Al(OH)3 precursor failed to completely transform into the rigid Al2O3 crystalline phase and still existed in the amorphous AlO(OH) intermediate state. At this point, the system was exposed to the air environment, and oxygen molecules interacted with the defective states on the surface of the CDs through a dynamic bursting mechanism, leading to a significant attenuation of their room-temperature phosphorescence signals. The intensity of phosphorescence emission from the 800-CDs@Al2O3 sample may have decreased as a result of the sharp reduction of luminescent centers brought on by the excessive consumption of R-CDs at high temperatures.

Investigations with varying temperatures were carried out to further validate the afterglow process of the CDs@Al2O3 powders. A characteristic of the weakening of the RTP radiation-free jump was the afterglow intensity and lifetime decreasing with increasing temperature. For CDs@Al2O3 composites, 500-CDs@Al2O3 and 600-CDs@Al2O3 had one main peak at 466 nm and 476 nm, while 700-CDs@Al2O3 had two main peaks at 475 nm and 525 nm at low temperatures (Fig. 5 a–f). Low temperatures slowed down the non-radiative jumps and prolonged the phosphorescence durations by reducing the thermal and vibrational movements of the CDs molecules. This confirmed that the afterglow of the composite material was a typical phosphorescence.

Additional research was done on the stability of CDs@Al2O3 phosphorescence. The protection provided by the Al2O3 matrix was responsible for the excellent stability of 500-CDs@Al2O3. As seen in Fig. 5g, 500-CDs@Al2O3 remained stable and its phosphorescence was visible to the unaided eye after being submerged in water, powerful oxidizing agents, polar solutions, and pH solutions for 21 days. As seen in Fig. 5h–i, their phosphorescence and phosphorescence duration were investigated. The results demonstrated that the R-CDs were encapsulated in the alumina matrix following the formation of covalent and hydrogen bonds with alumina. This prevented the afterglow from quenching and shielded the outer layer of the CDs with a rigid Al-O network, giving 500-CDs@Al2O3 a long afterglow lifetime and strong optical stability.

The excitation emission mechanisms of the three complexes were further determined. According to the following equation: [24]
(2)
Δ
E
ST
=
1240
λ
FL
-
1240
λ
PHOS

Using Eq. (2), the ΔE ST of 500-CDs@Al2O3, 600-CDs@Al2O3, and 700-CDs@Al2O3 were calculated to be 0.137 eV, 0.49 V, and 0.061 eV at 77 k at excitation wavelengths of 254 nm, 254 nm, and 280 nm. These low ΔE ST values indicated that the system favors intersystem crossover (ISC) and generated more excitons in the triplet state, thus creating suitable conditions for phosphorescence (Fig. 6 a–c). In afterglow luminescence systems, EPR spectroscopy was commonly used to verify the presence of electronic defects. [52,53] As shown in Fig. 6d, a paramagnetic absorption signal with a g-factor of 2.005 was observed in the CDs@Al2O3 composites, which was believed to be the result of a single electron occupying a paramagnetic oxygen vacancy. The results indicated that carbon atoms on the surface of R-CDs replaced aluminum atoms, and alumina formed covalent bonds with carbon atoms on the surface of R-CDs, leading to an increase in oxygen vacancy defects around R-CDs. This further proved that enhanced and densified Al2O3 and C-O-Al covalent bonding between CDs and Al2O3 were the causes of RTP.

Based on first-principles density-functional theory (DFT), the ground-state geometries of CDs were fully and optimally structured, and their HOMO-LUMO energy gaps were computed by Gaussian 09 procedure using B3LYP hetero-generalization and 6-31G(d) motifs [48]. The initial model of reduced carbon dots (R-CDs) constructed was a unilateral carboxyl-functionalized ternary ring structure, in which the carboxyl groups were anchored to the hydroxyl sites on the surface of AlO(OH) nanocomposites via hydrogen and oxy-aluminum ligand bonds to form a stable charge transfer channel at the Al-O-C interface. The strength of Al-O-C bonds between R-CD and the Al2O3 matrix increased with increasing calcination temperature (500–700 °C). The distribution of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for the three composite materials was shown in Fig. 6e. The ISC process was accompanied by the transition from HOMO to LUMO. Due to the increase in forging temperature, the CDs bonding points increase, causing the HOMO-LUMO energy gap to decrease from 5.59 eV in CDs to 5.20 eV in CD-Al2O3.This change indicated that calcination reduces the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of CDs-Al2O3, enhancing the orbital coupling of HOMO-LUMO, thereby promoting the ISC process. These numerical results were consistent with the trend of the ΔE ST values. Despite the fact that high-temperature calcination helped CDs and the Al2O3 matrix create covalent bonds, CD-Al2O3 progressively aggregated at higher temperatures, reducing its lifespan [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs and CDs @ Al2O3 composites
ce:section.ce:para:
The resulting CDs@Al2O3 could be used for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications due to their exceptional optical properties. A ‘CSUFT’ pattern was first formed on paper with 500-CDs@Al2O3, and the green phosphorescence would last for 5 s after the 365 nm UV lamp was extinguished (Fig. 7
a). The R-CDs and 600-CDs@Al2O3 powders were uniformly applied to the butterfly and flower moulds, and the butterflies disappeared immediately after switching off the UV light at 365 nm, and the brightness of the flowers decreased dramatically after a few seconds until they disappeared completely (Fig. 7b). The powder of 700-CDs@Al2O3 was applied to the bird mold, which emitted blue fluorescence under UV irradiation at a 285 nm wavelength, and the blue phosphorescence transformed into green phosphorescence until it disappeared with time (Fig. 7c). The novel applications of CDs@Al2O3 composite structural materials were thoroughly investigated. Since 500-CDs@Al2O3 had better phosphorescence than 600-Al2O3 and 700-CDs@Al2O3 to the unaided eye, it was used in the following application. Fingerprint detection technology was widely used in case investigations, security checks, etc. However, fingerprints left on the surface cannot be identified with the naked eye. Therefore, the use of fluorescent composites for fingerprint detection had attracted much attention due to their simplicity and low cost. Using 500-CDs@Al2O3 as the donor and rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) dispersed in the composite material, CDs/Rh6G@Al2O3 pink powder was prepared, which showed an orange fingerprint pattern under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7d and Video S1, Supporting information). In order to remove the interference of background fluorescence, 500-CDs@Al2O3 after irradiation at 365 nm, the fingerprints were clearly recognizable (Fig. 7e and Video S2, Supporting information). Similarly, 500-CDs/Rh6G was used as an anti-counterfeit QR code under laser light with phosphorescence emission long enough to scan the QR code and obtain the correct encrypted information under dark conditions (Fig. 7f and Video S3, Supporting information).

To develop the application even further, the researchers used 500-CDs/Rh6G@Al2O3 and 500-CDs@Al2O3 powders as inks and wove the ink-treated satin fabric material into a bow, which was found to emit bright fluorescence under 365 nm excitation and to emit excellent phosphorescence when removed (Fig. 7g), the excitation luminescence properties in an aqueous environment were also investigated, and this material still maintained good luminescence performance in water (Fig. S10, Supporting Information). In addition, nearly 1.35 million people died or became disabled in traffic accidents every year worldwide.[54] Although some traffic areas were equipped with relevant road signs or warning signs, these signs required advanced power supply equipment and lacked dynamic flashing effects. Based on this, an intelligent flashing traffic sign was fabricated. The ‘Watch out for Pedestrians’ traffic sign made of 500-CDs@Al2O3 was able to emit a long-lived afterglow under the illumination of car lights, as shown in Fig. 7h. If a car passes by the sign, the fluorescence emitted under the illumination of car lights could alert the car owner that the afterglow emitted by the sign for a short period of time may be captured by the next car after the car has left. Thanks to these unique RTP properties, CDs@Al2O3 composites were expected to be used in multimodal anti-counterfeiting, encryption, luminescent fabrics, and luminescent traffic sign lights.

In this work, environmentally friendly room-temperature phosphorescent materials that produced high stability and multiple emission modes were successfully prepared. CDs@Al2O3 exhibited excitation dependence, emitting green phosphorescence under 365 nm excitation, yellow phosphorescence under visible light excitation, and blue-green phosphorescence under sunlight excitation. CDs@Al2O3 exhibited an afterglow time dependence after the removal of 285 nm UV light, and its phosphorescence color changed from blue to green. In addition, the prepared CDs@Al2O3 showed good stability in acidic and alkaline solutions and polar solvents, etc., which were suitable for applications in harsh environments. DFT calculations and experimental results showed that the ΔE ST of CDs from the S state to the T1 state decreased with the increase of the bonding position between CDs and Al2O3, which enhanced the RTP emission with the increase of the calcination temperature. Based on the properties of CDs@Al2O3, patterns for multidimensional information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, and smart traffic signs had been successfully designed. This work offered a straightforward and adaptable approach for eco-friendly RTP materials with multi-mode emission, which facilitated the expansion of their application areas and the development of smart afterglow materials with richer colors, longer afterglow times, and visible-light excitation.

Min Cai: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhaoshuang Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Rongkang Chen: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yin He: Software, Formal analysis. Xu Xu: Methodology, Investigation. Xu-Min Cai: Validation, Conceptualization. Wei Yuanfeng: Visualization, Validation. Zhenfei Yang: Validation, Visualization. Chuntao Kuang: Visualization, Validation. Guoen Yang: Visualization, Validation. Yan Qing: Visualization, Validation. Xingong Li: Visualization, Investigation. Yiqiang Wu: Visualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors expressed their gratitude for financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (32201497), the science and technology innovation Program of Hunan Province (2023RC3158), and the Foundation of Jiangsu Province Biomass Energy and Material Laboratory (JSBEM-S-202302).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.163281.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High pH-induced efficient room-temperature phosphorescence from carbon dots in hydrogen-bonded matrices
